data.title: ENS founder warns of Google spoof that tricks users with a fake subpoena
data.description:
The founder and lead developer of Ethereum Name Service has warned his X followers of an “extremely sophisticated” phishing attack that can impersonate Google and trick users into giving out login credentials. 

The phishing attack exploits Google’s infrastructure to send a fake alert to users informing them that their Google data is being shared with law enforcement due to a subpoena, ENS’ Nick Johnson said in an April 16 post to X. 

“It passes the DKIM signature check, and GMail displays it without any warnings - it even puts it in the same conversation as other, legitimate security alerts,” he said. 

As part of the attack, users are offered the chance to view the case materials or protest by clicking a support page link, which uses Google Sites, a tool that can be used to build a website on a Google subdomain, according to Johnson. 

“From there, presumably, they harvest your login credentials and use them to compromise your account; I haven’t gone further to check,” he said.

The Google domain name gives the impression it’s legit, but Johnson says there are still telltale signs it’s a phishing scam, such as the email being forwarded by a private email address. 

Scammers exploit Google systems 

In an April 11 report, software firm EasyDMARC explained that the phishing scam works by weaponizing Google Sites.

Anyone with a Google account can create a site that looks legitimate and is hosted under a trusted Google-owned domain.

They also use the Google OAuth app, where the “key trick is that you can put anything you want in the App Name field in Google,” and use a domain via Namecheap that allows them to “put no-reply@google account as From address and the reply address can be anything.”

“Finally, they forward the message to their victims. Because DKIM only verifies the message and its headers and not the envelope, the message passes signature validation and shows up as a legitimate message in the user’s inbox — even in the same thread as legit security alerts,” Johnson said. 

Google deploying countermeasures soon 

Speaking to Cointelegraph, a Google spokesperson said they are aware of the issue and are shutting down the mechanism that attackers are using to insert the “arbitrary length text,” which will prevent the method of attack from working in the future. 

“We’re aware of this class of targeted attack from the threat actor, Rockfoils, and have been rolling out protections for the past week. These protections will soon be fully deployed, which will shut down this avenue for abuse,” the spokesperson said. 

“In the meantime, we encourage users to adopt two-factor authentication and passkeys, which provide strong protection against these kinds of phishing campaigns.” 

The spokesperson added that Google will never ask for any private account credentials — including passwords, one-time passwords or push notifications, nor call users.

data.title: Market Chatter: US Pressures Allies to Join Trade Crackdown on China
data.description:
The US is leveraging tariff negotiations to pressure allies into curbing trade ties with China, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

With numerous countries seeking relief from US import taxes, US officials are asking for concessions such as secondary tariffs and bans on Chinese goods, according to the report.

The push includes pressuring nations like Mexico to raise tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles, and lobbying countries in Europe and Asia to avoid becoming backdoor entry points for Chinese exports, Bloomberg wrote.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is spearheading the talks, aiming to form a united front to isolate Beijing economically, according to the report. But some allies remain wary, questioning both the strategy's success and the US' reliability.

A cohesive anti-China trade bloc remains uncertain, amid mixed signals from Washington and strained global ties, Bloomberg wrote.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Iron ore gains as focus shifts to favourable fundamentals
data.description:
Iron ore futures drifted higher on Thursday, as the focus shifted back to favourable fundamentals of firm near-term demand and lower supply, although intense trade tensions between the world's two largest economies capped gains.

The most-traded September iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) traded 0.99% higher at 713.5 yuan ($97.63) a metric ton as of 0256 GMT.

Earlier in the session, the contract hit 715 yuan, its highest since April 11.

The benchmark May iron ore (SZZFK5) on the Singapore Exchange was 0.57% higher at $98.75 a ton.

Inclement weather-induced lower supply in the past quarter, coupled with remaining firm near-term demand, supported prices of the key steelmaking ingredient.

BHP Group reported slightly lower third-quarter iron ore output due to cyclones. The other two giant miners Rio Tinto , and Vale reported lower first-quarter shipments and output, respectively.

Meanwhile, daily crude steel output among member steel mills was around 2.2 million tons in the first ten days in April, 3.4% higher than the prior ten-day period, data from the state-backed China Iron and Steel Association showed.

China's crude steel output in March jumped 4.6% to a ten-month high of 92.84 million metric tons.

Additionally, China's openness to trade talks helped boost sentiment across the commodity complex, ANZ analysts said in a note.

China on Wednesday unexpectedly named a new trade negotiator, a key appointment that some market participants interpreted as a positive signal to potentially ease the escalating tariff war with the United States.

Other steelmaking ingredients on the DCE moved sideways, with coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! down 0.98% and coke (DCJcv1) up 0.93%.

Steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange were mixed. Rebar ticked 0.1% higher, wire rod (SWRcv1) added 0.3%, stainless steel advanced 0.27% while hot-rolled coil shed 0.22%.

($1 = 7.3085 Chinese yuan)

data.title: U.S. Tariff Policy Poses Major Risk to Japan's Economy, BOJ Member Says
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

U.S. trade policy is one of the biggest risks to Japan's economic outlook, Bank of Japan board member Junko Nakagawa warned as she flagged continued uncertainty over tariffs.

"Since the extent of the [economic] impact largely depends on the future course of tariff policies, it is necessary to monitor developments with high vigilance," Nakagawa said in a speech to business leaders in Gunma prefecture, located in the center of Japan's main island.

Given the unpredictability of ongoing trade negotiations between the U.S. and Japan, many economists expect the BOJ to maintain its policy rate at 0.5% on May 1. ​In a Truth Social post earlier, Trump said it was a ​"Great Honor to have just met with the Japanese Delegation on Trade​," adding: ​"Big Progress!"

Japan's economy minister, who is in Washington for the talks, has said he is ready to listen to any requests, urging a "win-win" solution.

Nakagawa said higher U.S. tariffs will have a direct impact on Japanese corporate activity, negatively affecting exports, production, sales, capital expenditure and profits.

Trade restrictions could also weigh on the Japanese economy indirectly by deteriorating sentiment among businesses and households, or leading to wide fluctuations in commodity prices and financial markets, she added.

That view is in line with comments from BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda, who was testifying at a parliamentary committee meeting Thursday morning.

Signs of weakness in the economy could force the BOJ to pause, or slow, its attempt to raise interest rates further. On Thursday, Nakagawa reaffirmed the central bank's stance that it will continue to seek rate hikes if Japan's economy and price growth develop in line with projections.

For now, wage and price trends point to relatively bright domestic conditions in Japan. Major companies are giving employees another big pay raise this year.

"Upward pressure on wages could intensify further with labor market conditions remaining tight, and moves to reflect the past inflation and higher wages in selling prices could strengthen to a greater extent than expected," Nakagawa said.

Policymakers hope that wage growth will help improve sentiment among consumers, which has weakened due to persistent inflation.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: RBNZ's own preferred inflation model 2.9% for Q1 2025 (prior was 3.1% y/y in Q4 2024)
data.description:
Earlier today we had the official CPI data from NZ:

  • New Zealand Q1 CPI +2.5% y/y (expected +2.3%) & +0.9% q/q (expected +0.7%)

From the Reserve Bank of New Zealand now, its model falling below Q4 and under the top of its 3% band:

***

Reserve Bank of New Zealand's sectoral factor model of inflation, its preferred measure.

  • The model is based on the idea that inflation in each sector of the economy is influenced by a common set of underlying factors or "factors" such as changes in interest rates, exchange rates, and commodity prices.
  • The model describes how inflation in each sector of the economy is affected by the underlying factors. Estimates are derived from data on past inflation rates and other relevant indicators and are used to generate forecasts for future inflation in each sector.
  • The RBNZ sectoral factor model of inflation is particularly useful because it allows the central bank to identify the sources of inflationary pressures in different parts of the economy. For example, if inflation is rising rapidly in the housing sector, the RBNZ can use the model to determine whether this is due to changes in interest rates or other factors specific to the housing market. By understanding the sources of inflationary pressures, the RBNZ can adjust its monetary policy to target inflation effectively and achieve its inflation targets.

data.title: SPC Global Raises AU$20 Million via Fixed Rate Unsecured Notes Due 2029; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
SPC Global raised AU$20 million through the issue of fixed-rate unsecured notes with a four-year term and an interest rate of 9% per annum, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian bourse.

The proceeds will be used to repay senior debt, support growth initiatives, and for general corporate purposes, the filing said.

Settlement is expected to occur on Thursday, according to the filing.

Shares rose nearly 3% in morning trade Thursday.

data.title: MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA)
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 16, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA)

MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA)

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA)

APRIL 16, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA)

ARI MELBER, MSNBC

ARI MELBER: To get into this and other big stories, tonight, we have Congresswoman Linda Sanchez, Democratic California, who is also on the House Ways and Means Committee. Welcome.

REP. LINDA SANCHEZ (D-CA): Thank you.

MELBER: Great to have you. You have a lot of experience. Most administrations not only generally comply with court orders, but they always say they are trying. What does it tell you that the administration won't even request the return of this individual who is in the wrong place?

SANCHEZ: Yeah, well, I think Democrats have to face this hard truth that we are not dealing with a normal Republican administration. Every day, Donald Trump shows that he does not play by the rules. He tries to undermine our federal agencies, he tries to test the rule of law, and he tries to tear out the foundations of our democracy. So the fact that even the embassy in El Salvador, as I'm sure your work — Senator Van Hollen traveled to El Salvador, tried to meet with Mr. Garcia, was not allowed access.

The Embassy there says that the Trump Administration has done nothing to try to secure his release, even though they admitted they sent him, they deported him because they made an error, and in this country, one of the underpinnings is that you are allowed due process in this country. Whether you're citizen or not, you have certain rights before they can take away your right to freedom.

MELBER: Right.

SANCHEZ: And they have disregarded this, which is shocking. And as per usual, Trump says one thing and then does another. At first, he said, well, if the Supreme Court says, I have to get them back, I will. And now they are continuing to do nothing, and it's setting up a real showdown in terms of co-equal branches of government.

Now Donald Trump tries to grab power, he tries to exceed his authority. He tries to disregard the system of checks and balances that our country was built on. And so it's really a showdown between he and the judicial branch because let me tell you, the Congressional branch, which is ruled by the majority Republicans, are doing zero oversight.

MELBER: Right.

SANCHEZ: They are not holding him accountable for anything. So it's really the judicial branch that is the last standing check on his power.

MELBER: Right. And that's why we're all keeping an eye on what the Supreme Court does if they see that they're being flouted. There is push back, interestingly. I want to show some of what we seen, even in pretty red areas, on Republicans, on these very issues. Take a look.

SANCHEZ: Sure.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

CONSTITUENT: Are you going to bring that guy back from El Salvador?

PROTESTER: Free Kilmar. Free Kilmar.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: All right. Thank you.

CONSTITUENT: Do you support federal agents detaining students who are here legally?

PROTESTER: Free Kilmar. Free Kilmar.

REP. BRIAN MAST (R-FL): (inaudible) you will not be allowed in here.

CONSTITUENT: They're willing to let him do it whatever he wants us as POTUS, but some of the things he's doing are illegal.

CROWD: Yes.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MELBER: Does that encourage you? And do you think there might be a larger set of resistance that we're seeing, measured in many ways, than some in the beltway or perhaps even some of the Democratic Party leadership realize?

SANCHEZ: Yeah, what we're seeing is Republicans are so afraid to hear from their constituents, because their constituents get the basics that you can't just pick people up off the street, send them to a country into a maximum security prison, which is essentially akin to a death sentence without some kind of due process.

I mean, we have seen at Republican town halls, a few that have been held, constituents are angry. Now they make up all kinds of stories. They said, these are paid actors. These are activists that are busting from — these are their constituents, and they're angry to the point that Republicans are now hiding from their constituents.

They've been told not to do town halls, and so we have seen other people go in and do these kinds of town halls to listen to what people are upset about and to try to get them to channel that anger into positive actions that can lead to a change of what's going on in this country.

MELBER: Yeah. You also work on a lot of different issues. I mentioned Ways and Means. There's trade policy, tariffs. California, people sometimes forget the rest of the country is itself one of the largest economies in the world.

SANCHEZ: Yes.

MELBER: Here's a headline about it's actually trying to sue the administration over tariffs. It could lose billions from Silicon Valley, of course, where you have the Magnificent Seven and other companies that we hear about to agriculture. What do you see as the cost of this, of this Trump tariff war thus far, and what should be done?

SANCHEZ: Sure. Well, we have seen a completely chaotic plan by Trump. There's no cohesive strategy to what he's doing with tariffs or — or how to get us where we're supposed to be, and the fact that California is suing, I have to say again, the judicial branch, God bless them. They're the one check we still have on President Trump.

It's actually Congress' responsibility, according to Constitution, to have power over trade and tariffs, and he has really usurped that power by using emergency powers that are not appropriate in this case. So we are fighting back against that. California is a huge economy. We have a lot of businesses that are very dependent on Pacific Rim trade, as you mentioned, agriculture, Silicon Valley, so it has the potential to cost us millions, if not billions, of dollars if these tariffs go into effect.

They're going to affect consumers, they're going to increase the cost for consumers to buy goods, and with retaliatory tariffs like we are seeing with China, we are losing some of our biggest export markets for products that we produce, wines, etc. So it really has the potential to very much hurt California and the whole country. Now the reasons that Trump has given for these massive tariffs are number one, he says he wants to bring manufacturing back to the United States. Well, that's not going to happen overnight, and it's not going to happen without investments in workforce and facilities.

MELBER: Yeah, sure.

SANCHEZ: None of which Trump is doing.

MELBER: Right, it's sort of just a bullying of the world. There isn't like a plan on the home front.

SANCHEZ: Correct. And then the second reason he's given is because they need revenue. And why do they need revenue? Because they want to give massive tax cuts to people who earn over $400,000 a year. So they want to make consumers pay these higher costs so that they can give tax cuts to people who essentially don't need them.

So when they say, is this tariff going to be temporary? Is it going to be permanent? We budget tax bills on a 10-year window. So if you're relying on that revenue in order to pay for tax cuts, I'm not going to be a temporary tariff. You're looking at something that's probably long term, and long term is going to do huge damage to the United States.

MELBER: Yeah. It's an important warning. And you're in these issues, and you see it, and you — you know, there's been so many different crises that after the market slump, we don't know whether the pressure will continue. The business pressure was huge on Trump because people saw it as a failed tariff policy. Congresswoman Sanchez, our lead off guest tonight, thanks for joining us.

SANCHEZ: My pleasure.

END

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Wednesday at 11 PM ET
data.description:
Asian Stocks Rise as U.S.-Japan Trade Talks Kick Off

The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6%, with electronics and heavy industry stocks leading gains.

The Post-Covid Era of Ultra-Calm Markets Is Over

Investors are abandoning go-to strategies like "buy the dip" and snapping up bearish bets, bracing for more volatility.

ECB Is Poised for Seventh Rate Cut. Why It's Easing Faster than the Fed.

The European Central Bank is expected to reduce its benchmark lending rate for the seventh time since it started lowering borrowing costs in June.

Delisting Chinese Stocks Is a Real Possibility for Trump. There's a Lot at Stake.

Alibaba and Baidu, which both trade on the Nasdaq, have tumbled on worries that they could be banned from the U.S.

Foreign Central Banks Are Loading Up on T-Bills, Selling U.S. Bonds

Every month, the Treasury Department publishes delayed data on U.S. assets sold and bought by foreign central banks and investors.

Powell Warns of 'Challenging Scenario' for Fed as Trade War Rages

The central bank is wary of a situation where prices rise at the same time that growth slumps.

U.S. Tries to Crush China's AI Ambitions With Chips Crackdown

New chip restrictions for Nvidia and AMD show the Trump administration's determination to battle China on tech advances as well as trade.

Japan's Exports Rose in March, But Pace Slowed Sharply

Japan's exports rose for a sixth straight month in March but at a much slower pace as concerns over U.S. President Trump's tariffs start to weigh on global trade.

Bank of Korea Keeps Rates on Hold as Tariff Uncertainty Rises

South Korea's central bank held interest rates steady in April, taking a widely anticipated breather as it gauges how best to support an economy facing a severe hit from U.S. tariffs.

New Zealand's Annual Inflation Rises More Than Expected

New Zealand consumer prices rose more than expected in the first quarter, pushing annual inflation higher for the first time in almost three years just as the central bank faces tariff-driven economic uncertainty.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Wednesday at 11 PM ET
data.description:
Taiwan Semi Earnings Are Coming. Guidance Is Key.

TSMC is a crucial player in the artificial-intelligence chip market, which has been upended by tariff turmoil.

Alcoa to Face Steep Costs From Tariffs Despite Strong Results

The aluminum producer said it incurred about $20 million in first-quarter costs stemming from tariffs on imports. Alcoa's CEO said it would be impractical in the short term to increase U.S. aluminum production.

BHP's Third-Quarter Copper Production Rises, Iron Ore Output Flat

The world's No. 1 miner by market value reported a 10% rise in third-quarter copper output due to higher volumes from the giant Escondida mine, and flat iron-ore output.

Brunello Cucinelli Reports Revenue Growth, Confirms Guidance

The Italian luxury-fashion firm said its results were in line with expectations, and that revenue continued on a solid upward trend in the first half of April.

Moncler Beats Views but Signals Unstable Macroeconomic Environment

Moncler's update comes days after luxury bellwether LVMH reported lackluster results for the start of the year, setting a negative tone for the reporting season.

Nvidia Is Now the Biggest U.S.-China Bargaining Chip

The ban on sales of the H20 chips calls into question the company's ability to continually beat Wall Street's lofty expectations.

Hertz Shares Jump 56% as Billionaire Investor Bill Ackman Discloses Stake

Pershing Square Capital Management says it now owns about 4.1% of the company.

Heineken Tops Revenue View, Reaffirms Outlook Amid Tariff Concerns

The Dutch brewer backed its full-year guidance after quarterly performance beat market expectations, but warned that the consumer and geopolitical backdrop remains volatile.

Abbott Labs Bracing for Tariffs as Profits Rise

Abbott Labs boosted its earnings in the first quarter and hinted that its full-year guidance might have gotten a lift were it not for looming cost hikes from the recent tariff war.

Shipping Tycoons Discuss Separating Panama Ports From Hutchison Deal

Mediterranean Shipping Co. explores parallel tracks for contested Panama Canal ports and the rest of the deal with BlackRock.

data.title: Kina Securities Appoints Ian Clough as Chairman
data.description:
Kina Securities appointed Ian Clough as board chairman, effective Thursday, succeeding Isikeli Taureka, according to a same-day Australian bourse filing.

Clough is currently the executive chairman of Brian Bell Group and has served as a non-executive director of Kina since 2024.

The company's shares fell past 2% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Genesis Minerals Target Price Raised 1% to A$5.15/Share by Canaccord Genuity>GMD.AU
Fujitsu collaboration with Supermicro and Nidec to reduce data center energy consumption
data.description:
KAWASAKI, Japan, Apr 17, 2025 - (JCN Newswire) - Fujitsu today announced a collaboration with Supermicro Inc. and Nidec Corporation to improve data center energy efficiency. As part of this collaboration, the three companies will combine Fujitsu's liquid-cooling monitoring and control software, Supermicro's high performance GPU servers, and Nidec's high-efficiency liquid-cooling system to develop a service that will help data centers increase their power usage effectiveness (PUE). Fujitsu will assess the effectiveness of the solution at its Tatebayashi Data Center, aiming to help customers achieve a world-class PUE at their data centers by the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025.

Addressing the growing energy demands of data centers

The rapid proliferation of AI is driving a surge in computing demand, leading to a global increase in the number of data centers and their overall power consumption. Higher power consumption is largely due to the increased cooling requirements of GPUs used in AI servers. Many data centers utilize air cooling, resulting in high average PUE ratios. While liquid cooling offers significantly higher efficiency, the design, construction, and operation of liquid-cooling systems require a high level of expertise and skill, and deployment of these systems has been limited.

Roles and responsibilities

Fujitsu:

Apply its 40-year track record in liquid-cooling technology used in top-tier supercomputers and mission-critical servers around the world to develop software that provides real-time monitoring of liquid-cooled servers and optimizes cooling equipment. This is expected to reduce liquid-cooling equipment power consumption and improve overall data center energy efficiency by up to 40% compared to traditional air-cooling methods. The software will also centralize management of liquid-cooled servers and equipment, reducing data center management workload.

Supermicro:

Provide high-density, high-performance AI server systems optimized for liquid cooling. This eliminates the need for air cooling fans, significantly reducing server power consumption and improving the server environment with reduced noise and lower operating temperatures.

Nidec:

Provide highly efficient cooling systems centered around its globally recognized Coolant Distribution Units (CDUs), which have achieved cumulative shipments of 5,000 units as of November 2024. Nidec will utilize its proprietary pump technology and expertise in precision engineering to deliver reliable and high-performance liquid cooling solutions. These systems will optimize thermal management within servers, significantly reducing energy consumption and contributing to sustainable datacenter operations.

Fujitsu's liquid-cooling monitoring and control software will also be applied in a Fujitsu offering for datacenters that will be rolled out starting in Japan in Q1 fiscal 2025. The offering, tailored for customers who are considering liquid-cooling for their datacenters, will provide one-stop support for the entire liquid-cooling solution lifecycle, from the introduction and planning stages, to construction, operation and maintenance. It will be provided under a subscription model that can be deployed with low initial investment cost.

Fujitsu's Commitment to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)

The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by the United Nations in 2015 represent a set of common goals to be achieved worldwide by 2030.Fujitsu's purpose - "to make the world more sustainable by building trust in society through innovation" - is a promise to contribute to the vision of a better future empowered by the SDGs.

About Fujitsu

Fujitsu's purpose is to make the world more sustainable by building trust in society through innovation. As the digital transformation partner of choice for customers in over 100 countries, our 124,000 employees work to resolve some of the greatest challenges facing humanity. Our range of services and solutions draw on five key technologies: Computing, Networks, AI, Data & Security, and Converging Technologies, which we bring together to deliver sustainability transformation. Fujitsu Limited (TSE:6702) reported consolidated revenues of 3.7 trillion yen (US$26 billion) for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2024 and remains the top digital services company in Japan by market share. Find out more: www.fujitsu.com.

Source: Fujitsu Ltd

Copyright 2025 JCN Newswire . All rights reserved.

data.title: OCBC Uses Treasury Shares for Employees' Share Schemes
data.description:
Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp. or OCBC used another 108,910 treasury shares worth around SG$1.6 million for its employees' share schemes on Wednesday, a same-day Singapore bourse filing said.

Following the transaction, the number of treasury shares stood at 16,512,724 shares, the lender said.

data.title: Ellipsiz Increases Share Capital of Subsidiary
data.description:
Ellipsiz increased the share capital of its wholly-owned subsidiary, E Golfing, to SG$300,000 from SG$1, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Wednesday.

The increase was carried out through a subscription of 299,999 shares for SG$299,999.

The subscription will be used to fund the operations of the subsidiary's indoor golf simulator centers.

data.title: Evolution Mining Target Price Raised 6.9% to A$7.75/Share by Canaccord Genuity>EVN.AU
FNC INTERVIEW WITH KEVIN MCCARTHY (R) FORMER SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 16, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

KEVIN MCCARTHY (R) FORMER SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE

FNC INTERVIEW WITH KEVIN MCCARTHY (R) FORMER SPEAKER OF THE

HOUSE

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

FNC INTERVIEW WITH KEVIN MCCARTHY (R) FORMER SPEAKER OF THE

HOUSE

APRIL 16, 2025

SPEAKERS:

KEVIN MCCARTHY (R) FORMER SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE

LAURA INGRAHAM, FNC HOST

LAURA INGRAHAM: Joining me now, Kevin McCarthy, former Speaker of the House. Mr. Speaker, this narrative that they're trying to turn on the Republicans about immigration using words like Gestapo and people are being abducted, even American citizens could be at risk. Do you think that this is a successful way for the Democrats to reconnect with Middle America?

KEVIN MCCARTHY (R), FORMER SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE: Well, they lost the immigration issue very large in the last election, so now they're trying to reframe it. President Trump has now secured the border. So they want to change the topic. It's due process. You should be fearful even as an American. They want to confuse the issue, and it is talking points. You played an excellent clip where you showed a number of Democrats in different parts of the country on different shows, all using the same phrases, Gestapo, the scare tactics, pull up in vans, you know, like somehow a citizen should be afraid.

And they try to confuse people, not telling you that somebody is a murderer, someone is MS-13. No, they wave that all away, but that's really who they're working for. And this whole tour of AOC, it's all in congressional districts that they want to focus on to try to flip. So this is what they're trying to do, and they're trying to build for that case. The election is much sooner than people think. It's just around the corner.

INGRAHAM: Kevin, I want to talk about what Jerome Powell did today. He essentially came out and said that these tariffs which the Democrats are going to seize on, obviously, that the tariffs are going to, you know, likely to create some type of recession and that will hurt U.S. growth forecasts going forward, which I mean there is a — there is a — obviously, the Trump administration thinks the exact opposite about how these tariffs will work.

But of course, that sent the market way down, and you can kind of see this little game playing out between Powell and the Trump administration. Do you think the Trump administration needs to come out in a full-throated manner and refute what the Fed Chair said today about an issue that is still number one on the minds of all voters now and will be on the midterms.

MCCARTHY: Well, what he said is hurting the economy. The market reacts to what he said. Now, he only has a short amount of time left in this job, and I think there's some excellent people who could take this job going forward. But remember what President Trump has already done, if you want to look at pure economics, he's getting people to invest billions of dollars in America because of his policies, that's only going to grow the economy.

The Fed Chair should be working for America, not scaring America. And this is really out of the playbook of the Democrats. There's two major issues of why President Trump won, securing the border and economics, and they're trying to undercut those two fundamental issues. So the administration should be very bold and keep laying out exactly what they are doing. So soon as we get an agreement where I am watching negotiating with Japan and others, this will be opening up markets that have been closed for decades to American products.

Our markets are open up to them. Why is it wrong that we expect to be treated the same as we treat them?

INGRAHAM: Yes, Powell tiptoed right up to politics today, in my view. Mr. Speaker, thank you very much.

MCCARTHY: Thank you.

END

data.title: Asia Markets Signal Cautious OptimismMarket Talk
data.description: Asian markets appear to be cautiously optimistic, with investors staying on the sidelines ahead Friday's holiday across several markets, says Yeap Jun Rong, market strategist at IG. U.S. equity futures appear to be pointing toward some stabilization with a slight sense of calm, Yeap says. However, trade relations remain complex with a structural shift in geopolitics, "where countries are increasingly being forced to align with either the U.S. or China", Yeap says. Citing the export restrictions placed on some Nvidia chips, Yeap says that such a move reflects deeper issues in U.S. and China's bid for dominance in the economy and technology space, and "challenges that won't be resolved easily or quickly." (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: HK-listed Xin Point drops on flagging impact of US tariffs
data.description:
** Xin Point drops 7.6% to HK$2.91, lowest since August 22, 2024

** Stock set for biggest one-day pct drop since April 7

** Chineses automotive and electronic components maker required to pay 159% tariffs on current U.S. exports and says new tariffs will likely impact business and operations

** Co's direct shipments from China to the U.S. subject to tariff accounted for 30.7% of its total revenue for the year ended December 2024

** Says Group unable to concretely assess impact of new tariffs amid ongoing negotiations to pass tariff costs to end consumers and possibilities of relocating manufacturing to lower tariff regions

** YTD, Stock loses 27.3%

data.title: Treasury Wine Estates Outperforming Amid Declining Global Wine Demand, Jarden Research Says
data.description:
The global wine demand is declining due to US tariff uncertainty, and while Treasury Wine Estates' brands are not immune, they are outperforming in aggregate, Jarden Research said in a Monday note.

The investment and advisory firm cut its fiscal 2025 through to 2027 earnings per share forecast by 2% to reflect weaker US demand and sit below fiscal year guidance for group earnings before interest and taxes.

Jarden's earnings before interest and taxes estimate is at AU$769 million, below the company's guidance of roughly AU$780 million, the note said.

The challenge is a catalyst for the company to realize portfolio value, demerger potential, clarity on guidance, and the index exclusion key, according to the note.

The firm maintained Treasury Wine Estates' buy rating and AU$13.90 price target.

Treasury Wine Estates' shares were up almost 2% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Fitch Keeps OSB Group Ratings Unchanged on Strong Profitability
data.description:
Fitch on Wednesday confirmed its BBB long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook on OSB Group .

The rating agency said the British financial services group's ratings are driven by its strong profitability and capitalization, diversification plan and strong capital generation, among other reasons.

data.title: Singapore Post's Subsidiary to Become Sole Shareholder in Quantum Solutions International
data.description:
Singapore Post's subsidiary, SingPost Logistics Enterprise, entered into a deed of undertaking with Alibaba Investment (AIL), a subsidiary of Alibaba Group , to cease its shareholding in Quantum Solutions International (QSI), according to a Wednesday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The cessation of JV will end the company's joint venture with Alibaba Group in QSI, which will be executed through a selective capital reduction exercise.

Following the completion of the exercise, QSI will become a wholly owned subsidiary of the company, while the consideration payable to AIL will be SG$36.9 million.

data.title: China’s US bond holdings are going nowhere fast
data.description:
With China and the United States engaged in a full-on trade war, anxious investors are asking which side can press its financial advantage given Beijing's vast holdings of U.S. Treasuries. In truth the situation is more of a fraught equilibrium both sides have an interest in maintaining.

Commentators in Chinese state media have for years argued their government should use the debt to pressure Washington, whereas this week U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said those holdings - estimated at $1.1 trillion by Brad Setser of the Council on Foreign Relations - provide no leverage whatsoever.

Investors who watched a searing sell-off last week drive the benchmark 10-year yield <US10YT=TWEB> as high as 4.59% aren't so sure. Clients are peppering money managers with questions about a long-feared doomsday scenario: China weaponises its holdings, dumping some or all of them to push up American interest rates, either to gain leverage in trade negotiations or punish U.S. President Donald Trump for slapping triple-digit levies on Chinese goods.

These concerns have been heightened by a shift in recent years by Beijing to diversify foreign exchange reserves. Yet it needs a large pool of dollar debt on hand to sell if the yuan’s exchange rate starts tumbling. Granted, the People’s Bank of China has been wary of directly selling Treasuries to this end since 2015, when it burned through most of its reserves fighting runaway depreciation. But its alternative measures to manage its currency would pack little punch without the ultimate backing of the Treasuries holdings.

Even a limited paring down of these, delivered as a warning shot, would pose a serious danger. It would be difficult to arrange confidentially, and word of any sale would stoke fears of a total divestment and trigger a panic in global markets that would torch the value of China’s remaining dollar-denominated holdings. That would drive up the yuan’s relative value, delivering another blow to Chinese exporters.

Then there is the question of what China would do with the proceeds. Bessent has argued the Chinese central bank would have to buy the yuan, which would strengthen that currency - but it could simply hold the dollars accrued from Treasury sales instead. It could also opt to pile into other bond markets in Europe or Japan, though whether those governments would welcome purchases is another matter.

Yet, Bessent's broader point stands: weaponising Treasuries would be at best both dangerous and difficult for China. The gloves might one day come off if the People’s Republic lets its currency float freely, but President Xi Jinping has expressed a strong desire to keep the yuan stable. For now, China’s holdings of American debt will hamper more rapid financial decoupling - even if Beijing and Washington might prefer otherwise.

Follow @KangHexin on X

CONTEXT NEWS

Investors sold off U.S. Treasuries after President Donald Trump’s April 2 announcement of tariffs on global trading partners. The benchmark 10-year yield was trading at 4.3% on April 16, still elevated from pre-levy levels.

China is the second largest holder of American government debt after Japan, with official data showing direct holdings of $784 billion at the end of February. Brad Setser, senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations, estimates total Treasury holdings by the People’s Bank of China are closer to $1.1 trillion.

data.title: Zip Target Price Cut 4.5% to A$3.20/Share by UBS>ZIP.AU
Crewstone International and Vince Group Launch Groundbreaking RM150 Million Real Estate Investment Fund
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, MY / ACCESS Newswire / April 16, 2025 / Crewstone International Sdn Bhd, a PEMC-licensed private equity firm, and Vince Group, one of Malaysia's leading integrated real estate developers, are proud to announce the launch of a strategic partnership to establish a RM150 million Real Estate Investment Fund - marking a major milestone in the country's investment and property landscape.

This newly established fund will pioneer Malaysia's first fully integrated real estate investment ecosystem, enabling investors to participate in a seamless, end-to-end property investment platform - from acquisition and development to value enhancement and exit realization.

"This partnership sets a new benchmark for real estate investment in Malaysia. By combining our investment structuring capabilities with Vince Group's robust property ecosystem, we are unlocking a future where real estate investing is smarter, scalable, and more accessible," said Ahmad Izmir, CEO of Crewstone International.

The fund will focus on high-yield, risk-mitigated real estate opportunities throughout Malaysia, with an emphasis on capital preservation and attractive upside potential for both institutional and qualified investors.

"We're proud to collaborate with Crewstone to bring a bold vision to life - a one-stop, future-ready real estate platform designed to generate long-term value," said Dato' Vincent Nee, Group Managing Director of Vince Group. "This RM150 million fund is more than a financial vehicle - it's a revolution in how real estate investment is approached in Southeast Asia."

The signing ceremony, held in Kuala Lumpur, was attended by key stakeholders, and investors - marking the formal launch of this ambitious initiative. The partnership is expected to catalyze innovation within Malaysia's real estate and alternative investment sectors, while offering robust returns to aligned partners.

About Crewstone International

Crewstone International is a private equity and investment management firm licensed under the Securities Commission Malaysia (License No. VCPE/0099/2023). With over MYR 500 million in assets under management, Crewstone specializes in cross-border private equity, credit, and fund strategies with a focus on delivering value for institutional and government partners.

About Vince Group

Vince Group is a diversified property group with an end-to-end ecosystem across development, marketing, leasing, and hospitality. With a track record of transformative real estate projects across Malaysia, the Group is known for innovation, execution excellence, and a strong commitment to shaping the built environment.

data.title: FNC INTERVIEW WITH TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 16, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR

FNC INTERVIEW WITH TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

FNC INTERVIEW WITH TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR

APRIL 16, 2025

SPEAKERS:

TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR

LAURA INGRAHAM, FNC HOST

LAURA INGRAHAM: Joining me now, Trump border czar Tom Homan. Now Tom, if that is not good enough for Chris Van Hollen who traveled to El Salvador today to check on this aggrieved prisoner or the folks over at some of the other cable networks, then I guess they'll have proven all of our points tonight.

TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR: I mean Chris Van Hollen, the guy that didn't want to sign the Laken Riley Act that protects American citizens against illegal alien criminal gang members, who instead of protecting Americans, his constituents, his taxpayers, he goes to El Salvador and defends an illegal alien with a deportation order that's a member of MS-13, that's been designated a terrorist organization by President Trump. That's where he went to defend it. It's just disgusting.

The same senator who state yesterday released an illegal alien charged with murder, refused the ICE detainer hit the street. So rather than protecting his community, protecting the residents of Maryland, he goes to El Salvador to coddle an MS-13 gang member terrorist. It's ridiculous.

And also, I talked to an MS-13 expert today, who also showed me pictures of his hand where he says absolutely the tattoo in one of his hands was absolutely MS-13 gang tattoo. Nothing questionable about it. Two judges said he's MS-13 member, ICE says he's MS-13 member. Intelligence community says he's MS-13 member. El Salvador says he's MS-13 members. So, you know, it's just ridiculous.

And as far as all the people are speaking out against us, I cannot believe for a minute there's that many stupid people in the world and now able to see on them, smart meter, zero (ph). We'll give them that. But I can't believe these people really believe what they're saying. They got to be just pushing the narrative for political reasons. They can't be that dumb to think that this person is an MS-13 gang member and a public safety threat.

INGRAHAM: Tom, Karoline Leavitt, the White House Press Secretary yesterday said that he was also involved in human trafficking. Is there evidence of that, in addition to the MS-13 evidence?

HOMAN: There is information in some files that he may have been involved with ICE — in human trafficking. MS-13 are famous for human trafficking. MS-13 are famous for bringing little girls into our country, minors and prostituting them and put them in the force sex trade. They have also known in high schools to take young girls and bring them into the game and put them on the street to prosecute. That's one of the main reason — one main causes of their of their finances is prostitution, young girls across the United States,

MS-13 is in over 20 countries across the globe. They're actually in over, I think, 48 states, United States now, they're a big organization. But you know where they're not, you know they're not active, El Salvador because President Bukele shut them down. They don't exist on the streets of El Salvador.

INGRAHAM: Are you concerned about Judge Boasberg's contempt order and the discussion surrounding that given this consensus among the elite legal set Tom that you all are defying, essentially defying the Supreme Court here.

HOMAN: No. Look, I've been doing this job since 1984 and I worked for the greatest president in my lifetime. I don't care if people like me saying that or not. He proves it every day. We secured the border. Today, while I'm talking to you, the borders at the most secure in the history of this nation. He did that in seven weeks and Biden couldn't do it in four years.

We have arrested more criminal alien gang members, public safety threats and national security threats in two months than Joe Biden did in two years. No, I think we did the right thing. Law enforcement professionals at ICE, they swear under oath that this person is MS-13. Again, he got two federal judges saying he's MS-13. I think we did the right thing.

As far as the notion that, well, he had a withholding order not to remove El Salvador. He's not an illegal alien just — he's just not an illegal alien anymore. He's a designated terrorist. There's a different level of due process for terrorists. So I think we stand bar guns. I think we do — we did the right thing, and I think the Attorney General of the United States agrees that we did the right thing, so we'll stand by that.

INGRAHAM: All right. Tom, thank you, as always for joining us.

END

data.title: Ridley Target Price Cut 3.4% to A$2.80/Share by UBS>RIC.AU
NZ Dollar Falls Despite Strong CPI Data
data.description:
The New Zealand dollar slipped to around $0.591 on Thursday, snapping a six-day winning streak as higher-than-expected consumer inflation data failed to shift market expectations of further monetary easing.

New Zealand's annual inflation rate rose to 2.5% in the first quarter, slightly above market forecasts of 2.3%, from 2.2% in the previous quarter.

Despite the increase, the number is comfortably within the Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s 1–3% target range for a third consecutive quarter, reinforcing the view that it won’t prevent additional rate cuts.

Markets remain fully priced for another 25bps rate at the RBNZ’s next policy meeting in May, with rates seen potentially bottoming at 2.75% by year-end.

The currency also faced pressure from a stronger U.S. dollar after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the Fed would wait for more data on the economy's direction before making any changes to interest rates.

data.title: Novo Resources Starts Air Core Drilling at Western Australia Project in March Quarter; Shares Down 9%
data.description:
Novo Resources started air core drilling at the Balla Balla project in Western Australia in the March quarter, testing target zones of structural complexity and splay faults proximal to the Sholl Shear Zone, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

Additionally, a reverse circulation drill program is set to begin at the Tibooburra project in New South Wales later this month to test the gold mineralized trend, the filing said.

As of March 31, the company has a cash balance of AU$10 million and investments of roughly AU$35.6 million, per the filing.

The company's shares tumbled 9% in recent Thursday trade.
